title: Slicing and safe downcasts

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Slicing and safe downcasts; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: C++ Core Guidelines; living guidelines accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Slicing and safe downcasts
sources.claim: Copying a derived object into a base value slices derived state, while safe downcasts require a polymorphic hierarchy and a checked dynamic_cast when the dynamic type is uncertain.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Copying a derived object into a base value slices derived state, while safe downcasts require a polymorphic hierarchy and a checked dynamic_cast when the dynamic type is uncertain.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Passing Derived by Base value loses its added fields and override behavior, so the interface instead accepts const Base&. Your goal is to diagnose slicing and replace an unsafe cast with polymorphic behavior or a checked alternative. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Class interface and invariants; unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access; Virtual dispatch and abstract interfaces. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Copying a derived object into a base value slices derived state, while safe downcasts require a polymorphic hierarchy and a checked dynamic_cast when the dynamic type is uncertain. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: static_cast downcasts trust an external proof, dynamic_cast needs runtime type information and a polymorphic base, and reference failure throws rather than returns null. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: static_cast from Base* to Derived* checks the runtime object and returns null if the object is not Derived.

includes: Avoid object slicing and minimize checked downcasts through better interface design.
Diagnose slicing and replace an unsafe cast with polymorphic behavior or a checked alternative.
excludes: static_cast downcasts trust an external proof, dynamic_cast needs runtime type information and a polymorphic base, and reference failure throws rather than returns null.

outcomes: Diagnose slicing and replace an unsafe cast with polymorphic behavior or a checked alternative.

examples.title: Slicing and safe downcasts: defined case
examples.situation: Passing Derived by Base value loses its added fields and override behavior, so the interface instead accepts const Base&.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Passing Derived by Base value loses its added fields and override behavior, so the interface instead accepts const Base&.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Use dynamic_cast for a checked polymorphic downcast; static_cast performs no runtime type check and is valid only when the programmer already proves the dynamic type.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Use dynamic_cast for a checked polymorphic downcast; static_cast performs no runtime type check and is valid only when the programmer already proves the dynamic type.

examples.title: Slicing and safe downcasts: boundary case
examples.situation: A pointer dynamic_cast is checked for null before derived-only operations are used.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A pointer dynamic_cast is checked for null before derived-only operations are used.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: static_cast downcasts trust an external proof, dynamic_cast needs runtime type information and a polymorphic base, and reference failure throws rather than returns null.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: static_cast from Base* to Derived* checks the runtime object and returns null if the object is not Derived.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Diagnose slicing and replace an unsafe cast with polymorphic behavior or a checked alternative.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: static_cast from Base* to Derived* checks the runtime object and returns null if the object is not Derived.
misconceptions.correction: Use dynamic_cast for a checked polymorphic downcast; static_cast performs no runtime type check and is valid only when the programmer already proves the dynamic type.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: static_cast from Base* to Derived* checks the runtime object and returns null if the object is not Derived.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Slicing and safe downcasts: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
